comments.title: Banishing, Meditation, and Divination with severe physical disability
comments.content:
Apologies in advance for a slightly long explanation.

I have a friend from childhood who, truth be told, I didn’t know very well nor talk to very much, but nonetheless I had a huge crush on her from 3rd grade until around the end of middle school. A few months ago she got in touch with me out of the blue. She now has Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS), aka Lou Gehrig’s disease, a progressive and fatal neurodegenerative disease where the average life expectancy is 2 to 5 years, though there are outliers who last longer. (Steven Hawking lasted decades, but apparently he might very well be the longest living ALS patient who has ever existed on the planet.) She is right in the 2-5 year zone now, and there is a distinct sense that she is running out of time.  

Partly due to the fact that my daughter happens to also have a serious neurological disease, this woman and I have reconnected strongly and had a lot of conversation— sometimes a bit one-sided, as it takes a lot of effort for her, whether live or by mail. Given the strong feelings I had for her when I was a child despite hardly knowing her, on a hunch, I consulted with Geomancy as to whether we had some kind of karmic past life connection; I received one of the strongest “yes” answers I’ve ever had.

  She knows she is nearing the end, and for a while it seemed like she was ready to give up the ghost. Lately though I’ve been arguing that there may be a chance that if she starts to return to some serious inner work like she had starting doing at one point a while before these physical issues began, that her Higher Self might give her a reprieve of sorts and slow down the degeneration enough to give her some more time. I don’t know this to be the case for sure, but it does seem plausible to me given the timing at which she reconnected with me.

She has gradually become receptive to these ideas, even while her condition continues to deteriorate. The doctors are recommending that she stop breathing by herself most of the time and hook up to a machine for breathing— apparently by actively engaging her diaphragm, the lower motor neurons deteriorate further, so the machine is supposed to extend how much time she has left.

  It may be too late at this point, but still, in the hopes that embarking on a new spiritual journey might give her a sense of purpose and allow her to extend her life long enough to get some good work done in this body, I’d like to help her set up a feasible routine which fits within her physical limitations.

  1. Banishing Ritual. She cannot walk or move around very much, so a traditional SOP is out of the question for her. The Judson Exercise will also not work. In her case, would you recommend the Modern Order of Essenes version of the SOP? Or would you recommend an SOP done purely astrally? Or something else?  

2. Meditation. If she goes on machine-assisted breathing, should meditation line up with the pace of the machine’s breath?  

3. As for journaling or taking notes of her meditations, both speaking and writing are difficult for her at the moment. I am not sure that a computerized voice system which picks up her sub-vocalizations is within her price range, though I haven’t talked to her about it yet. If nothing like that is a feasible option, is there any kind of mental note-taking method you might recommend for her to try? Would you say the Art of Memory (performed using astral locations instead of physical ones) might be something worth trying in this regard? (I fear it might be a bit much for someone to tackle in her state, but I don't have too many other ideas.) Or, do you have any other suggestions for how she could keep a record with a minimum of writing?

4. Divination. She already has a fair amount of experience with astrology. Would you count that as a form of divination for the "banishing-meditation-divination" triumvirate?

replies.title: Re: Banishing, Meditation, and Divination with severe physical disability
replies.content:
1) I'd recommend the Essene version of the SoP. She can continue to do that no matter what happens to her body.

2) Yes.

3) I'd say don't worry about it. She needs a simplified set of practices that will work with her limits, and recordkeeping isn't essential.

4) Yes, very much so.

replies.title: Re: Banishing, Meditation, and Divination with severe physical disability
replies.content: Thank you very much, as always.

comments.title: Minerva again
comments.content:
Thank you to JMG and others who responded to my question about Minerva last week (https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/210855.html?thread=37422503#cmt37422503). I didn’t get to respond before the thread closed, but the replies were both interesting and heartening.

I took on JMG’s suggestion of finding a statue of Minerva, but found most of them either had Athena’s name on (does this matter? it doesn’t seem right) or they were quite horrible. Many showed her as willowy looking and distinctly underdressed, which felt cheap and disrespectful to such a strong goddess who is known for being chaste. Eventually, I came across the mosaic of Minerva designed by Elihu Vedder, who looks very interesting himself, which shows her looking strong and dignified, as well as fully clothed (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elihu_Vedder#/media/File:Minerva-Vedder-Highsmith.jpeg). I’ve printed it out, found a suitable frame and will keep it in the bedroom where I can commune quietly.

A couple of things this last week made me smile. I went for a rummage in a second hand shop and in one room there were figures of owls everywhere, clustered in groups or peeking out from shelves. Then, I’m reading Robert Harris’s ‘Pompeii’ and got to the part where the aquarius has asked Pliny for the loan of a ship. Guess what the ship’s name is – Minerva.

This is all a new experience for me and I’m enjoying it a lot. It’s also a real boon having somewhere to talk about it all, so thank you JMG.

replies.title: Re: Minerva again
replies.content:
I'm glad you posted again this week, as I thought of something that didn't come to me last week. The Soul's Inner Statues by Kaye Boesme is an introductory guide to the practicalities of worshipping many Gods. It's meant to be suitable for followers of any polytheistic faith, but it is grounded in the author's own viewpoint as a late classical Neoplatonist-inspired worshipper of the Greek Gods, so it ought to be quite compatible with developing a relationship with Minerva. It's available as a free ebook here: https://kayeofswords.github.io/soulsinnerstatues/

As for whether to treat Minerva and Athena interchangeably, welcome to one of the longstanding and ongoing challenges for polytheists! I don't have any definite answers for you, but I wanted to reassure you that whether similar Gods from different cultures are "the same" is something that folks have been wrestling with since at least antiquity. It sounds like your intuition has been steering you well, though, so if you don't find it an interesting question, I wouldn't worry about it too much.

Cheers, and luck in continuing to build this relationship,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Minerva again
replies.content: Thank you for the link to that ebook, jprussell. I'm several chapters in and it's a wonderful read so far.

replies.title: Re: Minerva again
replies.content: Very welcome! It was fellow regular hwistle who pointed me to it, so he should get the credit. I've only skimmed it so far, due to my resolution, but I'm looking forward to reading it in the new year.

replies.title: Re: Minerva again
replies.content: You're welcome!  If using art with the Greek name on it doesn't seem right to you, it's not right. Unfortunately there's a lot of butt-ugly Pagan art out there just now. The Elihu Vedder image is glorious, and should do very well.

replies.title: Re: Minerva again
replies.content:
Hey there! I saw you last week but was at work and too tired to reply at the time. If you want to go further and worship in the Roman tradition, this thread over on Sane Polytheism contains some good resources, the most long-winded of which are mine:
https://sanepolytheism.dreamwidth.org/1810.html

I'm not a devotee of Minerva, but I think she's very, very cool! I also know the statue you mention, and I thought it was rather disrespectful as well. I've also run into the problem of statues having the Greek names on them, which is simply because Greek stuff is more popular and in school everybody is told "The Roman gods are just rip-offs of the Greek gods" despite the existence of purely Roman gods like Janus and the Lares. I've emailed GreekArtshop (the Cretan creators of most of the great greek statues on the market) and they've said that though they can't change the old molds, all their new molds have no name on the base, so in the coming years some of their fine Athena statues may become useful for Roman polytheists. In the meantime, I wouldn't feel bad about printing out an image, since most Roman households had their Lares and household gods all painted into one small mural in an alcove on the wall.

Word of advice though, forgo candles in favour of handmade olive oil lamps made out of small metal lidded jelly jars - neither beeswax nor parafin candles are worth the hassle. They're never lit long enough during daily prayers to melt fully and they always tunnel, then the wick burns away or submerges itself shortly after being lit and it makes a mess.

Good luck, and may the gods shower you with their blessings and kind reassurance. <3

comments.title: Metals
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I had a few questions about planetary metals?

1) I know many metals were not discovered in ancient times, but is there any way to find out what planet/god, for example titanium, would be associated with by its properties? Or would it be a trial-and-error test of a ritual/working with each metal with each planet and see which 'works'?

2) Does an alloy retain the properties of all metals in it, or just the most dominant one? I know most alloys like stainless steel are modern, but bronze for example existed in the ancient world - was it viewed as being only copper-associated, or was the the tin also accepted as being part of it?
It's actually quite hard to get hold of most 'pure' metals these days, they're often either alloys or coatings. (And on that subject, would tin-plated steel be tin or iron-related?)

3) What would be an acceptable substitute for the metal mercury as an association with the planet Mercury? The metal is, for obvious reasons, hard to obtain and difficult to store/work with. I do realise that these properties are, of course, relevant to Mercury though...

Also, an observation - I think you mentioned iron having a powerful Martial association the other week - I assume that extends to steel too? I know I've felt a surge of what I can only call 'stabbiness' when picking up a kitchen knife sometimes.
And there's that whole expression of "Give a boy a hammer and the whole world is a nail" - I'm sure I can't be the only one who's felt the thrill of holding a sledgehammer or 5 pound lump hammer (both of which have cast iron/steel heads) and thinking "I wonder what it would do to that object..."

Thanks,
Mr Crow

replies.title: Re: Metals
replies.content:
1) It would take trial and error, involving experimentation and clairvoyant work. Some details have been worked out; it's fairly clear, for example, that aluminum corresponds to Uranus and nickel seems to be Lunar.

2) Neither one. Metallic alloys as a class correspond to Mercury -- you'll find old books that assign brass to Mercury, since that was the most common alloy back in the day.

3) See comment #2. I've used sheet brass for Mercury talismans with excellent results.

4) Steel is the great exception to the alloy rule, since it's an alloy of iron and carbon, and the latter isn't a metal. Steel is every bit as Martial as iron, and no question, you can tell. The energies of Mars aren't subtle.

replies.title: Re: Essene Healing Hands
replies.content: Some people, especially those with a strong oppositional streak, won't let themselves feel anything when doing exercises like this. I've found that this is much, much more common as a source of lack of psychic perceptiveness than any of the other causes. If you want to proceed with the practice, by all means; simply do seven slow breaths at each stage, and watch your emotional reactions. If you find yourself getting angry, frightened, or feeling some other emotional reaction that doesn't seem to have anything to do with the circumstances, you might consider journaling on the subject and seeing what childhood experiences, or what have you, might lie behind it.

replies.title: Re: Esoteric Baptism Link and Seven Laws Question
replies.content:
On historical grounds I am greatly inclined to doubt that Atkinson was ever a member of the H. B. of L., whether in its original form, or in its later offshoot, based in Denver. Of course, he may have read The Light of Egypt (1889; 2nd edition in 2 volumes, 1900), written by one of the two heads of the original H. B. of L., Thomas Henry Burgoyne. But that is hardly the same thing as being a member of the organization.

The Hermetic Brotherhood of Luxor (or Light) was active in the US only during the years 1884-1886. In 1886 it became widely known that Burgoyne had been convicted of fraud in 1883 and had spent a number of months in prison in consequence. (Burgoyne's original surname had been Dalton.) Most of the Brotherhood's early members resigned their membership in consequence of the public scandal.

During those years Atkinson was is his early 20s, and was busy establishing himself as a businessman and a lawyer in Pennsylvania, as well as courting the woman whom he would marry in 1889. He had not yet suffered the health crisis that led him to investigater New Thought doctrines toward the end of the 1880s

Despite the scandal and setback of 1888, Burgoyne did continue his work as a teacher of occultism, at first in Denver, CO, and then in (northern) California. He may have continued using the name of the H. B. of L. in his dealings with his relatively few students for a while. But Atkinson seems never to have lived in Denver, and to have visited (southern) California only briefly around 1903. So it seems unlikely that the paths of the two men would ever have crossed. And most of Atkinson's prodigious number of published books seem to have no real points of contact with either Burgoyne's books or the teachings of the H. B. of L. as we now know them from their publication in the book by Joscelyn Goodwin, Christian Chanel and John P. Deveney, The Hermetic Brotherhood of Luxor (1995).

Of course, the "inner circle of Pansophers" may simply be passing in all sincerity what someone else told them long ago.

replies.title: Re: Esoteric Baptism Link and Seven Laws Question
replies.content: Thanks for this! I also don't notice any significant overlap between Atkinson's work and The Light of Egypt, the later version of the HB of L material -- quite the contrary, that book (and the HB of L generally) reject reincarnation and have a lot of frankly rather snotty things to say about Indian teachings, where Atkinson is extremely comfortable with Hindu teachings and iirc affirms reincarnation.

replies.content:
I've heard scary warnings about burning your eardrum with melting wax.

Anyway, my own favorite way of cleaning the wax out of my ears, that I was raised with, is by using an "ear irrigation bulb" (search for it on https://start.duckduckgo.com/, you will get lots of hits!)

CAUTION: DO NOT USE THIS IF YOU HAVE A PERFORATED EARDRUM!

Fill the bathroom sink with tepid water, which feels neither hot nor cold to the touch.
Submerge the tip of the ear bulb and squeeze the air out of it, then un-squeeze it while its tip is still submerged to suck water into it. Hold it tip upright, gently squeeze til water comes out of the tip, then re-submerge it and release it to suck in some more water. It is now full.

Choose the first ear to be cleaned, tip your head so that that ear is facing downward, and locate it above the sink full of water.

Now stick the tip of the bulb just barely into your ear's opening. Do NOT jam it way in there. Put it just far enough in so that when you squeeze it, the stream of water is directed into your ear. Gently squeeze the bulb to squirt the water in. (If the water feels too hot or too cold, immediately stop and run more water into the sink to get the temperature just right.)

Squeeze the bulb until you have squirted out all the water you can; this will take about 30 seconds if you do it right. (Do not squirt your ear too hard!) Once emptied, re-submerge the bulb while it is still squeezed and refill it as above.

Repeat 3 or 4 times, sometimes you have to repeat a dozen times or so. Eventually, a big slug of earwax washes out into the sinkful of water. Rinse out your ear with another bulbful or two of water, and then that ear is done.

Drain the sinkful of water, refill with clean water of the right temperature, then repeat the above for the other ear.

When done, clean the ear irrigation bulb with mild soapy water inside and out, getting the inside by the squeeze-and-squirt method described above. Rinse with clear water the same way. In addition I like to suck a small dab of rubbing alcohol up into the bulb and swish it around in there to sanitize it for the next time.

Prop the ear bulb up onto a piece of tissue so that it is resting on its tip on the tissue, to drain out. Let dry overnight. Store in the medicine cabinet.
I have used mine for 40 years this way so far.

- Cicada Grove

I've had a somewhat odd hypothesis start to form in my head for what's happened to the Neo-pagan scene since the middle years of the 2000s, when I first started to notice the moral and intellectual decay that has created such a mess in the scene; this hypothesis explains why some people were strongly affected, while others were not.

Now, the hypothesis is that sometime in the later years of the 2000s, at least one god got offended by a common figure in the Neo-pagan scene, and decided to curse them. This figure is the kind of person who invokes the gods asking for goodies, and then refuses to offer anything in return; the kind of person who calls on the gods when in trouble and then ignores them the rest of the time; the sort of person who demands help from the gods and then refuses to listen to what they might say in response.

If this is what's happening, this would explain the way that some people in the Neo-pagan scene were utterly unaffected by the moral and intellectual collapse: by and large, the people who walked away from the scene as it fell further and further into demonolatry were those who either had positive relationships with the gods, or avoided dealing with them much. There were some who were into the Neo-pagan scene as a form of acting who walked out as it fell into the sleazy mess it became; others walked out as they noticed the spiritual energies of it were collapsing.

Many of those so cursed would have left the scene and become the kind of atheist who invokes demons solely to show the world they don't believe in them that became so common around the same time the Neo-pagan scene started it's slow descent into evil, while those who remained would corrupt the egregors of the organizations, and indeed the entire scene.

Meanwhile, the kind of person who would believe they could boss the gods around would be exactly the kind of person who would think they could order demons around safely, and this could easily explain the way that the scene collapsed so easily into demonolatry. And, of course, if this is the work of a god's curse, then it follows the gods would be willing to allow the demons a lot more leeway than normal in order to make the point for them, thus explaining the explosive growth in demonic activity lately....

Does this sound plausible to you?

comments.content:
Observations and ponderances about last week's disturbing question about the "contract for sale of soul":

1. However legally binding any contract may be, it can still be breached. That is, even if the person selling their soul agrees to not seek external aid and to resist any external aid rendered, they would presumably not be physically (or I guess astrally) compelled to do so. Breaching the contract in this way would presumably make the demon in ownership of the soul very, very angry (it would, in a sense, be property theft), but what power would they feasibly have in this instance? If the invading king's forces says to the farmer, "That deed you have that says this land is yours? It's ours now," what recourse do you actually have even if you have every legal standing on your side?

2. Since the renunciation of any entity who might potentially provide such aid is part of the contract, wouldn't it be feasible for such an entity who does not recognize the validity of the contract to also not recognize the renunciation, it being after all part of the contract?

3. Supposing that the contract is breached, help is obtained, and the signer-away has his/her soul returned to the cycle of reincarnation anyway, against the demon-owner's wishes and against the contract. What would we expect to see during that person's next life and next post-life?

4. Would it be possible to agree to such a contract by accident, without actual intent - for example, if it was secreted away in an otherwise innocuous contract, for instance, or would the requirements of the non-soul-pertinent terms of said contract render that difficult or impossible?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The contract can be breached. In Marlowe's Doctor Faustus, Faustus considers repenting, and Mephistopheles threatens him with instant death if he does so; if Faustus had gone ahead, especially with the help of clergy, his soul would have been saved, but he would have been killed by the devil. More generally, you can break such a contract, but only if you are willing to turn to a deity who redeems sinners of that kind, and if you do that, everything you gained by the demonic contract gets taken away, as painfully as possible. But you save your soul.

2) Yes, and that's also present in the traditional lore. Saints, angels, and deities, not being party to the contract, are free to exercise their capacities to rescue a repentant sinner. The sinner does have to repent, however.

3) That's a very good question not discussed in the literature I know. My guess, for what it's worth, is that they'd have to deal with repeated attempts to tempt them into doing the same thing over again.

4) It requires actual intent to make such a contract valid, but that intent can be casual, even joking.

replies.content: These are fine questions which I don't have the specialized knowledge to answer. Anyone else?

replies.title: Re: Minerva - Elihu Veddar
replies.content:
I do not have much in the way of specialized knowledge, just the fading memories of a B.A. in Classics, but a few thoughts:

1) I had a Latin professor in college who taught an optional lecture series on Etruscan architecture and statuary. The details are a bit fuzzy, but one of the things that stood out were that rooflines of temples often featured multiple statues of Gods/Goddesses. Doing a quick Brave image search doesn't reveal an especial preponderance of winged figures, but I notice what looks like a pretty clear Mesopotamian/middle eastern influence in sphinx-like and other winged figures. This would fit with when the Etruscans were their own thing, before the Greeks took over the Mediterranean world, which in classical and art history is called the "orientalizing" period, during which the cultures of the western Mediterranean (including Greek, Etruscan, and proto-Roman Italic folks) adopted a lot of imagery and artistic style from the Levant, which was at the time much richer and more sophisticated. All of which is to say there are a lot of fun avenues for exploration of winged statues if my suggestion below doesn't grab you.

2-3) The winged figure atop the globe(?) might be a representation of Nike, the Goddess of victory, due to the wings and the laurel crown in her left hand. I'm drawing a bit of a blank on what the thing in her right hand might be/represent - my first thought was a sheaf of wheat, but it could be a feather as you say or perhaps a palm frond. Palm fronds were also sometimes used as symbols of victory, as featured in the Palm Sunday story, and sneaking some Christian imagery into an otherwise pagan-inspired piece of art would be a typical Victorian move. Nike was famously seen as an aspect/hypostasis of Athena by the Greeks, as in the "Athena Nike" statue from the Acropolis. For an American artist of the late 19th/early 20th century to use the Roman name but Greek imagery would also not be unusual at all for someone born in the Victorian era, as the Victorians largely accepted the late Roman view of accepting the Greek Gods as identical to their Gods they identified with them (so, "Minerva = Athena").

4) If you're willing to expand the scope of stories to include Athena, Minerva/Athena were associated with the Aegis, a protective piece of mythical something. The various traditions about what the Aegis might be (a helm with characteristics of a Ram or a shield bearing the severed head of Medusa) are reflected in the mosaic. Minerva/Athena was also protective of her chosen warriors (Achilles and Odysseus, along with Odysseus's son Telemachus) - maybe most vividly portrayed in the Iliad when Athena grabs Achilles by the hair to stop him from starting an ill-advised fight with Agamemnon, the high king of the Greeks besieging Troy. Perhaps unfortunately, we don't have much in the way of "purely Roman" stories about Minerva that don't show conscious influence from the stories about Athena. Both seem to have been associated with the strategy and discipline of ordered fighting (the phalanx in Greece and early Rome, the distinctly Roman styles of legion fighting later), which might also have some "protective" overtones.

5) Incidentally, the Latin at the bottom is apparently from Horace's Epistles, and translates as "Not unwilling, Minerva raises a monument more lasting than bronze", according to wikipedia

Cheers and good luck digging in,
Jeff

replies.content:
Not specifically in their "planetary" sense, but a few works on Greek/Roman myth you may find helpful:

Hesiod's Theogony and Homer's Iliad and Odyssey were considered by the ancient Greeks and Romans some of the most foundational works on the Gods. Add in the Homeric Hymns, the Aeneid, and Metamorphoses and you'll have very many of the stories that you may find recounted in the summaries/scholarly work below.

Edith Hamilton's Greek Mythology is readable and meant for a general audience, but has serious scholarly chops. Greek Religion by Walter Burkert focuses more on what we know about ancient Greek worship, but includes a discussion of the mythology as well. I've heard good things about Stephen Fry's Mythos, but I haven't checked it out yet.

Hope these help!
Jeff
